pattern: Deleting up to k elements from a window to make everything left equal only costs you 'window size minus how many of the majority value are already in that window' -- so growing a window while tracking the best single-value frequency seen so far, and shrinking only when that cost exceeds k, finds the answer in one linear sweep.
hints: For any window of the array, the cheapest way to make it all-equal is to keep the most frequent value in that window and delete everything else -- so the cost of a window is its length minus its most frequent value's count.

Slide a window across the array with two pointers, keeping a running count of how many times each value appears inside the window, and track the best (highest) single-value count you've seen in the current window.

Grow the window one step at a time; whenever (window length) minus (best single-value count so far) exceeds k, shrink from the left by one instead of resetting -- the best single-value count never needs to decrease, because the largest window you can ever justify is exactly that best count.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, delete two to get three equal 3s
statement example, delete two to get four equal 1s
all-distinct values with k at minimum, no deletions help
k equal to the whole array length still can't beat the best single frequency
already all equal, zero deletions needed
alternating values where deleting the minority reaches the full array
two values tie for most frequent, best window uses only part of the array

stress.description: long arrays drawn from a small value alphabet so runs and repeats are common
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 100000))
    domain = max(1, min(5, n))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, domain) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(0, n)
    return [nums, k]
